food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked crab dish with egg-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general crab food product class (FOODON_03302757); no specific salted egg crab entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked crab generically; no specific salted egg sauce preparation exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 covers crab as a food item generically; no salted egg crab preparation code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 1 whole crab (~500 g edible portion including shell)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one whole crab of approximately 500 grams as served at a tze char restaurant, yielding roughly 200–250 g of edible crab meat and sauce combined.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked — nutrient composition adapted with salted egg sauce components; primary anchor: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (NDB 15139); salted egg sauce adjustment based on USDA FoodData Central, Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (NDB 01129) and butter/evaporated milk components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for salted egg crab exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139) as the protein/fat/mineral base with a proportional salted egg sauce contribution (salted duck egg yolk, butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, chilli, garlic). The dish is approximately 55–60% crab meat and 40–45% sauce by edible weight. Macros are blended accordingly. Sodium is elevated due to salted egg yolk content. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a restaurant-prepared portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes; analogous estimate based on cooked crab and egg-based sauce (very low carbohydrate content)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg crab. GI is estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of protein- and fat-dominant seafood dishes with minimal carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g ~3.2 per 100g). Cooked shellfish and egg-based preparations consistently fall in the GI 10–20 range. Glycemic load per 500g serving = (15 × 16g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.4, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg yolk and sauce preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salted egg crab due to its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100g, equating to approximately 2,600 mg sodium per 500g serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted egg crab has a very low carbohydrate content (~3.2g per 100g) and an estimated glycemic index of 15, resulting in a negligible glycemic load per serving; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the high sodium and saturated fat content from the salted egg sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and cholesterol from salted egg yolk and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The salted egg sauce contributes significant saturated fat (~4.8g per 100g) and cholesterol (~145 mg per 100g) from duck egg yolk and butter, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Crab is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean tze char dish consisting of a whole fresh crab — typically mud crab or flower crab — cooked in a rich, creamy salted duck egg yolk sauce. The sauce is prepared by stir-frying mashed salted duck egg yolks with butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, chilli, and garlic in a wok over high heat until a thick, golden, foamy coating forms. The crab is then tossed through the sauce until fully coated. The result is a dish with a deeply savoury, umami-rich, slightly briny flavour from the salted egg yolk, balanced by the creaminess of butter and evaporated milk and the aromatic heat of curry leaves and chilli. The texture contrasts firm, sweet crab meat with a velvety, clingy sauce. Nutritionally, salted egg crab is high in protein (~13.5g per 100g) and moderate in fat (~9.2g per 100g), with a significant saturated fat contribution from the egg yolk and butter. Sodium content is notably high (~520 mg per 100g) due to the preserved salted egg yolk, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus from the crab meat. It is typically served as a shared main course at tze char restaurants and seafood establishments across Malaysia and Singapore, often accompanied by steamed white rice or mantou (fried buns) for dipping.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for salted egg crab exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked crab data with proportional salted egg sauce component contributions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database classification framework for protein-dominant seafood dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the component sources are approved.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139) combined with proportional salted egg sauce components (salted duck egg yolk, butter, evaporated milk). No direct approved-source entry for salted egg crab was found. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crab source entry and cannot be reliably estimated for the sauce components without a direct measurement. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy crab source and the sauce contribution is not quantified in approved sources. 4. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous classification for protein- and fat-dominant seafood dishes with minimal carbohydrate content (~3.2g per 100g). 5. Glycemic load per 500g serving calculated as (15 × 16g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.4. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain non-halal crab preparation methods depending on the restaurant; consumers should verify with the specific establishment. 7. The serving size of 500g refers to the whole crab as served; edible yield (meat plus sauce) is approximately 200–250g. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate.
